V The Helvetii, despite his death, continued to pursue their original plan: leaving their territories. When they thought they were at length prepared for this undertaking, they set fire to their towns, numbering about twelve, and to their villages, which numbered around four hundred. They also burned up all the remaining private dwellings. The corn was destroyed, except for what they intended to carry with them. This act would serve as a final blow to their hope of returning home, making them more determined to face all the dangers that lay ahead. They persuade the Rauraci, Tulingi, and Latobrigi, their neighbours, to adopt the same plan. After burning down their towns and villages, they set out with them. The Boii, who had dwelt on the other side of the Rhine and crossed into Norican territory, join their party as confederates and assault Noreia.

VI There were two routes they could take to leave their country: one through the Sequani, which was narrow and challenging, lying between Mount Jura and the Rhone River. This path was difficult due to a steep mountain that overhung, making it easy for a few people to intercept them. The other route was through our province, which was much easier and less obstructed. The Rhone River flowed between the territories of the Helvetii and Allobroges, who had recently been subjugated. In some areas, the river was crossed by a ford. They believed they should either win over the Allobroges, since they had not yet shown any strong affection for the Roman people, or force them to comply by using military power to allow them to pass through their territories. VIII Meanwhile, with the legion he had with him and the soldiers who had assembled from the Province, he carried a wall for nineteen miles, to a height of sixteen feet, and a trench, from the lake of Geneva, which flows into the river Rhone, to Mount Jura, which separates the territories of the Sequani from those of the Helvetii.

Dumnorix, due to his popularity and liberality, held great influence among the Sequani. He was also friendly towards the Helvetii because he had married the daughter of Orgetorix from that state. Driven by a desire for sovereignty, Dumnorix sought a revolution and wished to attract as many states as possible through his kindness towards them. He therefore undertakes the affair and prevails upon the Sequani to allow the Helvetii to march through their territories. He arranges that they should give hostages to each other—the Sequani not to obstruct the Helvetii in their march—and the Helvetii are to pass without mischief or outrage. The Centrones and the Graioceli and the Caturiges, having taken possession of the higher parts, attempted to obstruct the army's march. After having routed these in several battles, he arrives in the territories of the Vocontii in the Further Province on the seventh day from Ocelum, which is the most remote town in the Hither Province. He then leads his army into the country of the Allobroges and subsequently to that of the Segusiani.

At the same time, the Ambarri, friends and kinsmen of the Aedui, informed Caesar that it was not easy for them now that their fields had been devastated to defend against the enemy's violence in their towns. The Allobroges, who had villages and possessions on the other side of the Rhone, fled to Caesar and assured him they had nothing left except the soil of their land. Caesar, driven by these circumstances, decided he should not wait for the Helvetii to arrive after they had destroyed all the property of his allies among the Santones. When Caesar was informed by spies that the Helvetii had already conveyed three parts of their forces across the Rhine River, but that the fourth part was left behind on this side of the Saône, he set out from the camp with three legions during the third watch, and came up with that division which had not yet crossed the river.

In this Caesar avenged not only the public but also his own personal wrongs. The Tigurini had slain Lucius Piso, the lieutenant of Cassius, who was the grandfather of Lucius Calpurnius Piso, Caesar's father-in-law. This occurred in the same battle as Cassius himself. "The Helvetii had been so trained by their ancestors that they were accustomed to receive, not to give, hostages. The Roman people were witnesses to this fact." "He had withdrawn." The Helvetii, elated by the outcome of the battle, began to face us more boldly. They had previously repelled a large body of horse with five hundred horses, and this success emboldened them to launch attacks from both front and rear.

Caesar restrained his men from battle, considering it enough to prevent the enemy from plundering, foraging, and pillaging. XVI Caesar continued to press the Aedui daily for the corn they had promised in the name of their state. The delay was due to the cold weather, which made the corn in the fields unripe and left insufficient fodder in store. Furthermore, Caesar was unable to use the corn he had transported up the River Saône because the Helvetii, who were reluctant to leave, had diverted their march from the river. He [Caesar] makes inquiries privately about the same points with others and discovers that it is true. He learns that Dumnorix is the man in question - a daring individual who enjoys great popularity among the people due to his generosity. Dumnorix has been secretly negotiating with the Aedui for years, securing customs and taxes at a low cost because no one dares to compete with him when he makes an offer. One thing stood in the way of all this: that he had learned from experience his brother Divitiacus's high regard for the Roman people, his great affection towards them, his distinguished faithfulness, justice, and moderation. He was afraid that by punishing him, he might hurt his brother's feelings.

During the third watch, he ordered Titus Labienus, his lieutenant with praetorian powers, to ascend to the highest ridge of the mountain with two legions, accompanied by those who had previously examined the road. He then explained his plan. He himself hastened to them during the fourth watch, taking the same route that the enemy had used, and sent all his cavalry ahead of him. Publius Considius, a seasoned veteran of military affairs, having served under both Lucius Sulla and Marcus Crassus, was dispatched ahead with the scouting party.

XXII At daybreak, when Titus Labienus held the summit of the mountain, Caesar was no more than a mile and a half from the enemy's camp. Neither had his arrival nor Labienus' been detected, as Considius later learned from captives. Just then, Considius rode up to him on horseback, saying that the mountain Caesar wanted Labienus to capture was now in enemy hands. He claimed this discovery came from observing Gallic arms and banners. Labienus, as he had been ordered by Caesar not to come to an engagement unless his own forces were seen near the enemy's camp, waited with his men on the mountain, taking possession, while refraining from battle. The Helvetii, either because they thought the Romans, struck with terror, were retreating from them, or because they flattered themselves that they might be cut off from supplies, altered their plan and changed their route, began to pursue and harass our men in the rear.

He himself drew up a triple line of his four veteran legions on the middle of the hill. Above him, he placed the two recently levied legions from Hither Gaul and all the auxiliaries on the very summit. He ordered that the entire mountain be covered with men, while the baggage was brought together into one place, and the position was protected by those in the upper line. The Helvetii, following their wagons, gathered their belongings into one location. They then, after repelling our cavalry and forming a tight formation, advanced towards our front line in a dense formation. XXV Caesar, having removed his own horse and those of his soldiers from view, made the danger more equal for everyone and eliminated the possibility of escape. He then rallied his troops and joined the battle.

It was a great hindrance to the Gauls in battle that when several of their bucklers had been pierced by one stroke of Roman javelins and pinned fast together, they could neither pluck it out nor fight with sufficient ease, even with their left hand entangled. He did this chiefly because he was unwilling that the country from which the Helvetii had departed should be uninhabited. This was to prevent the Germans, who lived on the other side of the Rhine, from crossing over into the lands of the Helvetii and becoming neighbors of the province of Gaul and the Allobroges. He granted the petition of the Aedui, that they might settle the Boii, in their own territory. e In the Aeduan territories, as these were known to be of distinguished valour, they were given lands, and subsequently admitted to the same state of rights and freedom as their own people. After years of violent struggle between them for supremacy, the Germans were hired by the Arverni and Sequani.

e The Germans had initially crossed the Rhine, but after that, these wild and savage men became enamored with the lands, refinement, and abundance of the Gauls. As a result, more were brought over, and there were now as many as 120,000 Germans in Gaul. The Aedui and their dependents repeatedly clashed with these newcomers in battle, only to be defeated and suffer a great calamity. They lost all their nobility, senate, and cavalry. And though such engagements and calamities had broken them, although they had formerly been very powerful in Gaul, both from their own valour and from the Roman people's hospitality and friendship, they were now compelled to give the chief nobles of their state as hostages to the Sequani, and to bind their state by an oath, that they would neither demand hostages in return, nor supplicate aid from the Roman people, nor refuse to be forever under their sway and empire. But a worse thing had befallen the victorious Sequani than the vanquished Aedui. For Ariovistus, the king of the Germans, had settled in their territories and seized a third of their land, which was the best in all Gaul. Now he ordered them to leave another third part, for just a few months earlier, 24,000 men from the Harudes tribe had come to him, requiring room and settlements.

XXXIV He therefore decided to send ambassadors to Ariovistus to demand that he name an intermediate location for a conference between the two. He wished to discuss state business and matters of great importance to both parties. Caesar warned Ariovusthat he would not overlook the wrongs of the Aedui. He replied that no one had ever entered into a contest with him without suffering utter ruin. He had provided a supply of corn as quickly as possible and hastened to Ariovistus by forced marches.

Caesar hastens to the town by forced marches both night and day, and after seizing it, he stations a garrison there. They threw down their javelins and fought with swords hand to hand. Although the army of the enemy had been routed on the left wing and put to flight, they still pressed heavily on our men from the right wing. This circumstance afforded Caesar no less pleasure than the victory itself; for he saw a man of the first rank in Gaul, his intimate acquaintance and friend, rescued from the enemy's hand and restored to him. Fortune had not diminished the joy and exultation of that day by his destruction.

As he became aware that all the forces of the Belgae, gathered in one location, were approaching towards him, he learned from scouts he had sent out, as well as from the Remi, that they were not far away. He then hastened to lead his army across the Aisne, which lies on the borders of the Remi, and pitched his camp there. This position secured one side of his camp near the riverbanks, rendering the country behind him safe from the enemy. Provisions could now be safely transported to him by the Remi and the other states. The Gauls' method of besieging is similar to that of the Belgae. When a large number of men are gathered around the entire fortifications, stones begin to be cast against the walls on all sides. The defenders are stripped from their positions, and they form a testudo, advancing towards the gates to undermine the wall. This was easily accomplished on this occasion, as the sheer size of the force casting stones and darts made it impossible for anyone to maintain their position on the wall.

Therefore, after staying a short time before the town, they laid waste to the country of Remi. When all the villages and buildings within reach had been burnt, they hastened with all their forces to Caesar's camp and encamped within less than two miles of it. Their camp, as indicated by smoke and fires, extended more than eight miles in breadth. VIII Caesar initially decided not to engage in battle, partly due to the large number of enemy forces and their renowned reputation for bravery. Nevertheless, he continued to test the enemy's capabilities through repeated cavalry actions, seeking to determine how far they could push their skills and what his own troops were willing to endure. He left the two legions he had recently raised in the camp, reserving them for future use as a reserve. With this done, he arranged the remaining six legions in battle formation before the camp.

The enemy hastened from that place to the river Aisne, which was behind our camp. Caesar, immediately learning this through his scouts, feared an ambush because he had not yet discovered why they were leaving. He kept his army and cavalry within the camp.

Attacking their rear and pursuing them for many miles, our soldiers slew a great number of them as they fled. Those who had come up with them halted bravely and sustained the attack. The van, appearing to be removed from danger, broke its ranks as soon as the noise was heard, resting on flight to ensure their safety. He had attempted to capture it by force on his march, as he believed it was poorly defended. XXIII The soldiers of the ninth and tenth legions, stationed on the left flank of the army, swiftly drove the Atrebates from the higher ground into the river. The Atrebates, exhausted from running and wounded, were unable to resist. As they attempted to cross the river, the Roman soldiers slew a large number of them with their swords while they were hindered by the water. XXV Caesar proceeded, after encouraging the tenth legion, to the right wing. There, he observed that his men were hard-pressed and that the concentration of the twelfth legion's standards in one location hindered their own fighting efforts due to overcrowding. The loss of centurions from the fourth cohort was catastrophic, with all but a few being killed. The standard-bearer was also slain, and the standard itself was lost. Many centurions from other cohorts were either wounded or killed, including the chief centurion of the legion, Publius. XXIX When the Aduatuci, of whom we have written above, were coming with all their forces to assist the Nervii in battle, upon this being reported to them, they returned home after being on the march; deserting all their towns and forts, they gathered together all their possessions into one town, which was eminently fortified by nature.

While this town surrounded it with high rocks and precipices on all sides, one side offered a gentle ascent, no wider than 200 feet. They fortified this approach with a double wall, making it very lofty, and placed heavy stones and sharpened stakes along its walls. After the destruction of their homeland, these people had been subjected to harassment by their neighbors for many years. At one point, they launched an offensive war, while on another occasion, they resisted such a war when it was waged against them. In the end, they reached a peace agreement with all parties involved and chose this location as their new settlement. XXX They made frequent sallies from the town upon our arrival, engaging our men in brief skirmishes. Later, when surrounded by a 12-foot-high rampart that encircled them for 15 miles, they confined themselves to the town.

The soldiers cast a large quantity of their arms from the wall into the trench before the town. The heaps of arms nearly reached the top of the wall and rampart. Despite this, they retained and concealed about one-third of their arms in the town. The gates were then opened, and they enjoyed peace for that day. They, by design before entering, as we later understood, had believed that, having surrendered, our men would dismiss their guards or keep watch less carefully. This was partly due to the arms they had retained and concealed, and partly because of shields made from bark or woven wicker, which they quickly covered with animal skins. In the third watch, they suddenly launched a surprise attack on the town with all their forces, aiming for our fortifications, whose approach seemed the most feasible. Caesar, after breaking open the gates, sent in his soldiers.

Caesar ordered all embassies to return to Rome at the beginning of the following summer. They despised a single legion because it was small in number, with only two cohorts present and several individuals missing who had been sent to secure provisions. Similarly, they believed that due to the unfavorable terrain, even our initial attack would be unsustainable when they launched a counterattack from the mountains into the valley, firing their weapons at us. Our men, initially, while their strength was fresh, fought bravely and did not cast any weapon uselessly from their elevated position. V When they had been fighting for over six hours without cessation, their strength and even weapons began to fail. The enemy pressed on more rigorously, demolishing the rampart and filling up the trench as our men grew exhausted. The situation had reached its final extremity.

As he assembled the centurions, he quickly gave orders to the soldiers to discontinue the fight for a short time, collect the weapons that had been thrown at them, and rest after their fatigue. VII When these things were achieved, Caesar had every reason to suppose that Gaul was at peace. The Belgae had been overcome, the Germans expelled, and the Seduni defeated among the Alps. With this accomplished, he set out for Illyricum in the beginning of winter, intending to visit those nations and acquire a knowledge of their countries. However, a sudden war broke out in Gaul.

VIII The influence of this state is by far the most considerable of any country on the whole sea coast. The Veneti have a very great number of ships, with which they have been accustomed to sail to Britain, and excel the rest in their knowledge and experience of nautical affairs. As only a few ports lie scattered along that stormy and open sea, they hold as tributaries almost all those who are accustomed to trade in that sea. X There were several difficulties mentioned earlier in carrying on the war, but many factors still prompted Caesar to continue the conflict. The open insult offered to the state by detaining Roman knights, the rebellion that arose after surrendering, and the revolt that followed when hostages were given, were among these factors. Additionally, the confederacy of so many states was a significant consideration. Most importantly, Caesar was concerned that if this part of the war was overlooked, other nations might interpret it as permission for them to take similar action. He orders him to visit Remi and the other Belgians, and to maintain their loyalty and prevent the Germans from crossing the river by force if they tried to do so.

XII The sites of their towns were generally such that, being placed on extreme points of land and on promontories, they neither had an approach by land when the tide had rushed in from the main ocean. This always happens twice in the space of twelve hours; nor by ships, because upon the tide ebbing again, the ships would be dashed upon the shoals. They sailed more easily during most of the summer because their ships were often delayed by storms, making it difficult to navigate the vast and open sea, which featured strong currents and scarce harbors. XIII Their ships were built and equipped in that manner.

XIV Caesar, after taking many of their towns, realized that such a large amount of labor was being wasted and that the enemy's flight could not be stopped once their towns were captured. He also understood that no further harm could be inflicted on them. With this in mind, he decided to wait for his fleet to arrive. Our men had the advantage of courage in the rest of the contest. This was particularly true because the entire action took place in the sight of Caesar and the entire army. As a result, any act that showed more valour than usual could not go unnoticed, since all the hills and higher grounds, which offered a clear view of the sea, were occupied by our army. XV The sail-yards of the enemy, as we have said, being brought down, although two and three ships of theirs surrounded each one of ours, the soldiers strove with great energy to board the ships of the enemy. After the barbarians observed this taking place, as many of their ships were beaten, they hastened to seek safety in flight.

And having now turned their vessels to that quarter in which the wind blew, a great calm and lull suddenly arose, making it impossible for them to move out of their place. This circumstance proved exceedingly opportune for finishing the business, as our men gave chase and took them one by one. As a result, very few of them survived, with those who did arriving at land after the battle had lasted almost from the fourth hour until sunset. They accordingly surrendered themselves and all their possessions to Caesar. On this, Caesar thought that punishment should be inflicted the more severely, in order that for the future, the rights of ambassadors might be more carefully respected by barbarians. And in those few days, the Aulerci and the Sexovii, having slain their senate because they would not consent to be promoters of war, shut their gates against us and united themselves with Viridovix. A great multitude of desperate men and robbers also assembled from Gaul, drawn by the hope of plundering and the love of fighting, which had lured them away from their daily work and husbandry. Sabinus kept himself within his camp, which was conveniently located for all purposes. Viridovix encamped opposite him at a distance of two miles, daily bringing out his forces and giving Sabinus an opportunity to fight. As a result, Sabinus had not only gained the enemy's contempt but also been subjected to taunts from our soldiers' speeches, which fueled suspicion of his cowardice. This led the enemy to presume they could approach as close as the rampart of our camp. He adopted this conduct because he did not believe a lieutenant should engage in battle against such a large force, especially when the commander-in-chief was absent, unless it was on favorable ground or under circumstances that made it advantageous to do so.

XVIII He had suspected cowardice in him, so he chose a skilled Gaul, one of the soldiers he had brought along. Many things persuaded the Gauls to take this measure: Sabinus's delay during previous days; a positive assertion from a pretended deserter; lack of provisions, for which they had not taken adequate precautions; hope stemming from the Venetic war; and also because in most cases men willingly believe what they wish. He ordered a sudden sally to be made from two gates of the camp while the enemy was hindered by the weight of their burdens.

It happened due to the enemy's unskilfulness and fatigue, as well as our soldiers' valour and experience from previous battles. They could not withstand even one attack from our men and immediately turned their backs. Our men, though disordered, followed with full vigour and slew a large number of them. The horse pursued the rest, leaving only a few who managed to escape by flight. At the same time, Sabinus was informed of the naval battle and Caesar's victory. All states immediately surrendered to Titurius because the Gauls' temperament is impulsive and prone to war, yet their minds are weak and unable to endure hardships. Crassus, upon his arrival in Aquitania (which, as previously noted, accounts for a significant portion of Gaul's territory and population, making it roughly one-third of the region), recognized that he would be waging war in this area. A few years prior to his arrival, Lycius had been defeated by the Arverni.

Our men were attacked while disordered, and they renewed the fight. They once attempted a sally, but also formed mines along our rampart and vineyards (where the Aquitani are particularly skilled, as copper mines can be found in many places among them). When they realized that these efforts would not yield any benefits through their men's persistence, they sent ambassadors to Crassus and begged him to accept their surrender. But then, the barbarians, alarmed by reports that we had captured a town fortified by its natural defenses and our own artistry, within just a few days of our arrival, sent ambassadors to all regions to form alliances, exchange hostages, and mobilize troops.

Although outnumbered due to their great number and ancient renown in war, and our limited men, they believed it was safe to engage in battle. However, they deemed it wiser to secure victory without sustaining any injuries by besieging the passes and cutting off supplies. If the Romans, weakened by a lack of grain and facing retreat, they planned to launch an attack while their forces were burdened and demoralized. Crassus, having observed this circumstance, realized that the enemy was intimidated not only by their own delay but also by the reputation of Rome. e For cowardice arising from this event had rendered our soldiers more eager for fighting. The remarks of all were heard [declaring] that no longer should delay be made in going to the camp. After encouraging his men, he marched to the camp of the enemy, which was met with great gratification by his own troops. The cavalry pursued them across the open plains. After leaving only one-fourth of their original force of 50,000 men, who had assembled from Aquitania and the Cantabri, they returned late at night to the camp.

A few remote nations, depending on the time of year, would often neglect to prepare for winter. XXVIII About the same time as Caesar, although the summer was nearly past, he led his army to Gaul since all of Gaul had been reduced. The Morini and Menapii alone remained in arms and had never sent ambassadors to him to make a treaty of peace. They quickly moved their army towards him, thinking that this war might soon be brought to an end. When Caesar arrived at the start of these forests, he began to fortify his camp. There was no sign of enemy activity in the meantime. Our men were busy with their assigned tasks when they were suddenly attacked by a force that emerged from all parts of the forest. The latter quickly took up arms and drove them back into their forests; and having killed a great many, they lost a few of their own men while pursuing them too far through those intricate places.

XXIX During the remaining days, Caesar began to clear the forests. To prevent an attack on the flank of his soldiers, who were unarmed and unaware of the threat, he placed all the cut-down timber opposite the enemy and built a rampart on both flanks. Caesar, having ravaged his entire country, burned down villages and homes, led his army back to its winter quarters among the Aulerci and Lexovii, and stationed them alongside the other states that had waged war against him previously. They are said to possess 100 cantons. From each of these cantons, they annually send 1,000 armed men to their territories for the purpose of war. The remaining cantons support both themselves and those engaged in the expedition.

They were in arms a year later. The previous ones stayed at home. II Merchants have access to goods rather than having persons to whom they can sell the items they have acquired through war, or because they require specific commodities to be imported to them. The Germans do not use imported cattle, but rather the poor and deformed animals native to their country. These, however, are made capable of great labor through regular exercise.

They do not permit wine to be imported to them, for they believe that men become weaker in their ability to endure fatigue and are made more effeminate by it. III They esteem it their greatest praise as a nation that the lands surrounding their territories lie unoccupied to a very great extent, because they believe this circumstance indicates that many nations cannot withstand their power. On the other side of the Suevi, the lands are said to be desolate for approximately six hundred miles. On the other side, they bordered on the Ubii, whose state was large and flourishing, considering the condition of the Germans. They were somewhat more refined than those of the same race and the rest of the Germans. This was due to their proximity to the Rhine, which made them a hub for merchants, and as a result, they had adopted the manners of the Gauls.

Although the Suevi, after repeatedly attempting to dislodge this nation from their territories through frequent wars, were ultimately unsuccessful due to the vastness and size of their state, they still managed to make them vassals, rendering them significantly weaker and less influential than before. Having slain those and seized their ships, they crossed the river before reaching the part of the Menapii who lived in peace in their settlements along the Rhine. It was not until they had informed them of their intention that the Menapii were aware; and seizing all their houses, they maintained themselves on their provisions for the rest of the winter. Caesar summoned the chiefs of Gaul and, in a display of humility, pretended not to know about the discoveries he had made. He won them over by confirming his previous claims and then ordered the formation of a cavalry unit. With their minds now at ease, Caesar decided to wage war against the Germans.

When he was distant from them only a few days' march, ambassadors arrived from their state. Their speech was as follows: "The Germans do not make war on the Roman people first, nor do they decline to engage with them in arms if provoked. This is the custom of the Germans, passed down from their ancestors, to resist any people who make war on them and not to avert it by entreaty." However, they admitted that this was not the case, as they had come here reluctantly, having been expelled from their own country. Caesar stated that he could not provide them with anything; he had discovered that they had dispatched a significant portion of their cavalry across the Meuse River to the Ambivariti region several days prior, with the intention of plundering and acquiring forage. Caesar believed that these things converged on the same point [as their other proposal]; [namely] that, due to a three-day delay, their horse, which were at a distance, might return. However, he stated that he would not proceed further than four miles that day in order to obtain water. He ordered them to assemble at that location the following day, with as many people as possible, so that he could investigate their demands.

XII But the enemy, seeing our horse, numbered 5,000, attacked us despite having only 800 horses. Those that had crossed the Meuse to forage had not returned, while our men were undaunted, as their ambassadors had left Caesar earlier that day and had requested a truce for that very day. While endeavouring to render assistance to his brother, who was surrounded by the enemy and whom he rescued from danger, he himself was thrown from his horse, which was wounded beneath him. Nevertheless, the horse continued to oppose its antagonists with great intrepidity as long as it was able to maintain the conflict. As he lay surrounded by enemies on all sides, wounded multiple times, his brother, who had retreated from the battle, watched from a distance. Seeing his brother's peril, he urged his horse forward, leapt onto the enemy lines, and was cut down.

XIII.--After this engagement, Caesar considered that neither should ambassadors be received to audience, nor conditions be accepted by him from those who, after having sued for peace by way of stratagem and treachery, had made war without provocation. He therefore deemed that no time for converting measures ought to be afforded them. He then drew all his forces out of the camp and commanded the cavalry to follow in the rear because he thought they were intimidated by the late skirmish.

Our soldiers, after the alarm of such a great war, returned to their camp, all safe to a man, with only a handful being even wounded. They, fearing revenge and torture from the Gauls, who had harassed their lands, wished to stay with him. The portion of the cavalry from the Usipetes and Tenchtheri tribes, which I previously mentioned as having crossed the Meuse to plunder and gather forage, was not present at the engagement. After their countrymen retreated, they took the opportunity to cross the Rhine into the territories of the Sigambri and joined forces with them.

Although the greatest difficulty in forming a bridge lay before him, due to the river's breadth, speed, and depth, he nonetheless believed it should be undertaken by him, or else his army would not have been led across. After he had sunk the engines into the river and fixed them at the bottom, he then drove them in with rammers, not quite perpendicular to the riverbed. Instead, they were angled forward and sloped, inclining in the direction of the river's current. He also placed two additional piles opposite these, forty feet lower down, fastened together in the same manner but directed against the force and current of the river. XVIII Within ten days of commencing timber collection, the entire project was finished, and the entire army was marched out.

Therefore, after summoning merchants from all corners, he could learn nothing about the island's size, the number of nations that inhabited it, their military tactics, their customs, or which harbors were suitable for numerous large vessels. Having given them an audience, he promised liberally and exhorted them to continue in that purpose. He then sent them back to their own country and dispatched with them Commius, a man whose courage and conduct he greatly esteemed. He had created Commius king upon subduing the Atrebates, and believed him to be faithful and of high influence in those countries. XXII While Caesar remains in these parts for the purpose of procuring ships, ambassadors come from a great portion of the Morini to plead their excuse regarding their conduct on the late occasion. They allege that it was as men uncivilized, and those unacquainted with our customs, that they made war upon the Roman people, promising to fulfill what he commands. Caesar, thinking that this had happened fortunately enough for him, because he neither wished to leave an enemy behind nor did he have the opportunity to carry on a war due to the time of year. He therefore took a large number of hostages and when they were brought before him, he received them under his protection.

While our men were hesitating [whether they should advance to the shore], chiefly on account of the sea's depth, he who carried the eagle of the tenth legion, after supplicating the gods that the matter might turn out favourably for the legion, exclaimed, "Leap, fellow soldiers, unless you wish to betray your eagle to the enemy." I will fulfill my obligations to the community. When he had spoken with a loud voice, he leaped from the ship and bore the eagle towards the enemy. Our men, however, were unable to maintain their formation or establish a solid footing. As individuals from different ships gathered around various standards, they became disoriented and lost their way.

The enemy, familiar with the shallow waters, would charge at any approaching vessel one by one, urging their horses forward and attacking them while dismounted. Many surrounded a small group, while others hurled their arms at our concentrated forces on their vulnerable flank. Our men, once they had established a firm footing on dry ground and all their comrades had joined them, launched a surprise attack on the enemy, forcing them to retreat. However, they were unable to pursue them far due to the horse's inability to maintain its course at sea and reach the island. XXVII The enemy, having been defeated in battle, immediately sent ambassadors to Caesar to discuss a peace treaty. With him arrived Commius, an Atrebatian, whom I have previously mentioned as having been sent by Caesar to Britain.

They had seized him when he left his ship, despite bearing the general's commission as an ambassador. After the battle, they sent him back, and in seeking a peace treaty, they blamed the incident on the common people, asking for forgiveness due to their impulsive behavior. Caesar complained that after they had sued for peace and voluntarily sent ambassadors to the continent for that purpose, they had made war without cause. He agreed to pardon their mistake and imposed hostages, giving some immediately. The rest he said would be given in a few days, as they were sent from remote places. A great many ships having been wrecked, as well as the rest, having lost their cables, anchors, and other equipment, were unfit for sailing. This led to a great confusion throughout the army, as there were no other ships available to transport them back. Moreover, all necessary items for repairing vessels were in short supply, and corn for the winter had not been stockpiled in those areas, since it was widely assumed they would spend the winter in Gaul.

They entered into a conspiracy once more and began to leave the camp in stages, secretly bringing their people from the countryside. XXXI Caesar, though he had not yet discovered their plans, sensed that something was amiss due to the damage to his ships and the failure to deliver the promised hostages. His suspicions proved correct as events unfolded. He therefore provided remedies against all contingencies. He daily conveyed corn from the countryside into the camp, using the timber and brass from severely damaged ships to repair the others, and ordering any other necessary items to be brought to him from the continent.

And thus, since the business was executed by the soldiers with great energy, he managed to make a voyage possible despite the loss of twelve ships. For as all the corn had been reaped in every part, except for one, the enemy suspected that our men would return to it and had concealed themselves in the woods during the night. Meanwhile, while these events were unfolding and our men were engaged, the rest of the Britons who were in the fields departed.

XXXV Although Caesar anticipated that the same thing would happen again if the enemy was routed, they would escape danger by their speed. However, having obtained about thirty horse, which Commius, the Atrebatian, had brought from Gaul, he drew up the legions in order of battle before the camp. Caesar doubled the number of hostages he already held, then ordered them to be transported across the continent. He made this decision because the approaching equinox meant he didn't want to risk the journey with his ships in disrepair; it was better to wait until winter. Having met with favorable weather, he set sail a little after midnight. All his fleet arrived safely on the continent, except for two ships of burden that couldn't reach the same port as the others, and were instead carried slightly lower down.

After our cavalry came into view, the enemy, realizing they had no advantage, threw down their arms and turned their backs. A large number of them were then killed. Cotta, who had led the legions into the territories of the Menapii, had laid waste to their lands, cut down their crops, and burned their homes. He then returned to Caesar because the Menapii had all fled into their densest forests. BOOK V I Lucius Domitius and Appius Claudius were consuls. When Caesar departed from his winter quarters in Italy, as was his custom, he ordered the lieutenants he had appointed over the legions to ensure that as many ships as possible were built during the winter, and the old ones repaired.

For dispatching cargo and drawing it ashore, he lowers his ships slightly more than our usual vessels; this is even more so because he knows that frequent tidal changes result in fewer swells. He achieves this to facilitate the transportation of small numbers of horses alongside larger quantities. He himself, upon his return from the assizes in Hither Gaul, proceeded to Illyricum, for he had learned that the region closest to them was being ravaged by the Pirustae's incursions. When he arrived there, having surveyed the winter quarter, he found that, despite the soldiers' extraordinary enthusiasm, amidst extreme scarcity of all materials, about six hundred ships similar to those described above, and twenty-eight warships, were nearly ready for launch.

He left a sufficient number of soldiers for that design; he himself proceeds into the territories of the Treviri with four legions without baggage, and 800 horse, because they neither attended the general diets in Gaul nor obeyed his commands, and were also said to be secretly collaborating with the Germans beyond the Rhine. IV Caesar, though he discerned from what motive these things were said and what circumstance deterred him from his meditated plan, still, in order that he might not be compelled to waste the summer among the Treviri while all things were prepared for the war with Britain, ordered Indutiomarus to come to him with 200 hostages. In the same place, the cavalry of the whole of Gaul assembled, along with the chief persons of all the states. He had determined to leave only a few of them behind, whose loyalty towards him was clear, and take the rest as hostages. This decision was made because he feared a commotion in Gaul when he should be absent. He had particularly decided to take him with him, for he had found him to be fond of change, fond of power, possessing great resolve and considerable influence among the Gauls.

To this was added that Dumnorix had previously stated in an assembly of the Aeduans, that the sovereignty of the state had been conferred upon him by Caesar. The Aedui bore with impatience and yet dared not send ambassadors to Caesar for the purpose of either rejecting or deprecating [that appointment]. He initially sought to remain in Gaul through every appeal. He did so partly out of fear of the sea, as he was unfamiliar with it, and partly because he claimed that divine warnings had forbidden him from leaving. VII Caesar, having learned this fact, decided that since Dumnorix had conferred so much honour upon the Aeduan state, he would take measures to restrain and deter him by whatever means possible. Given his increasingly erratic designs, Caesar deemed it necessary to exercise caution lest Dumnorix might cause harm to himself and the commonwealth.

Therefore, having stayed about twenty-five days in that place, because the north wind, which usually blows a great part of every season, prevented the voyage, he exerted himself to keep Dumnorix in his allegiance. He then learned all his measures. Having at length met with favourable weather, he ordered the foot soldiers and the horse to embark in the ships. While the minds of all were occupied, Dumnorix began to depart from the camp towards home with the cavalry of the Aedui; Caesar was unaware of this. Caesar, upon this matter being reported to him, ceased his expedition and deferred all other affairs. He sent a great part of the cavalry to pursue him and commanded that he be brought back. If he used violence and did not submit, he was to be slain. Caesar considered that Dumnorix would do nothing as a rational man while he himself was absent, having disregarded his command even when present. He, however, when recalled, began to resist and defend himself with his hand, and implored the support of his people, often saying he was free and the subject of a free state. They surround and kill the man as they had been ordered; but the Aeduan horsemen all return to Caesar.

All the ships reached Britain nearly at mid-day; nor was there seen a single enemy in that place. However, as Caesar afterwards found from some prisoners, large bodies of troops had assembled there. But being alarmed by the great number of our ships, more than 800 of which - including those from the previous year and private vessels each had built for their own convenience - had appeared at one time, they had quitted the coast and concealed themselves among the higher points. IX Caesar, having disembarked his army and chosen a convenient place for the camp, discovered from the prisoners that the enemy's forces had lodged themselves in what part. He left ten cohorts and 300 horse by the sea to guard the ships, then hastened to the enemy at the third watch. Caesar forbade his men from pursuing them beyond a short distance. He did so because he lacked knowledge of the terrain and wanted to conserve daylight to complete the fortification of their camp.

As they advanced a little further, when already the rear of the enemy came into view, some horse arrived from Quintus Atrius to report that on the preceding night, a very great storm had arisen. Almost all the ships were dashed to pieces and cast upon the shore, as neither the anchors and cables could withstand the force of the storm, nor could the sailors and pilots endure its violence. He chooses skilled laborers from his army and instructs others to be dispatched from the continent. He sends a message to Labienus, ordering him to construct as many ships as possible using the legions that are currently with him. He spent about ten days on this matter, with the soldiers working tirelessly, even at night.

It is unlawful to consume the hare, cock, and goose. However, these animals are bred solely for entertainment purposes. Immediately after this retreat, the auxiliaries who had assembled from all sides departed. The enemy never engaged with us in very large numbers afterward. The capital town of Cassivellaunus was not far from there. It was defended by woods and morasses, and a large number of men and cattle had been gathered there. The Britons, when they have fortified the intricate woods, in which they are wont to assemble for the purpose of avoiding the incursion of an enemy, build a defensive structure with an entrenchment and a rampart, and thus create what they term a "town". Thither he proceeds with his legions. He finds the place admirably fortified by nature and art. He, however, undertakes to attack it in two directions. XXII While these things are happening in those places, Cassivellaunus sends messengers into Kent, which is on the sea. Over it, four separate kings once ruled: Cingetorix, Carvilius, Taximagulus, and Segonax. He commands them to gather all their forces and suddenly attack and storm the naval camp.

When they arrived at the camp, our men, after launching a surprise attack, killed many of their enemies and captured a prominent leader named Lugotorix, bringing their own troops back to safety. He decided to send his army back by two separate voyages. XXIV The ships having been drawn up and a general assembly of the Gauls held at Samarobriva, because the corn that year had not prospered in Gaul by reason of the droughts, he was compelled to station his army in its winter-quarters differently from the former years. He distributed the legions among several states: one of them he gave to Caius. He himself remained in Gaul until he had established a secure presence and knew that his winter quarters were well-fortified.

To Caesar, he had restored the position of his ancestors, in consideration of his prowess and attachment towards him, as a result of which he had utilized his valuable services. Lucius Plancus, fearing that several individuals involved in the act might provoke the state into revolt, ordered a legion to proceed swiftly from Belgium to the Carnutes. He intended to spend the winter there and then arrest those responsible for his death, with the goal of identifying the person who had orchestrated it. XXVI About fifteen days after they had come into winter-quarters, Ambiorix and Cativolcus, who had met with Sabinus and Cotta at the borders of their kingdom, induced by the messages of Indutiomarus, one of the Treviri, excited their people. They suddenly assailed the soldiers and engaged in procuring wood. A large body then came to attack the camp.

XXXIII Titurius, with no prior preparation, became confused and ran about, attempting to arrange his troops. However, he did so with great trepidation, as if all his options were dwindling. This was a common phenomenon among those who had to make decisions on the spot, without having time to gather their thoughts or seek advice. Lucanius, fighting valiantly alongside his son, is slain as they are surrounded by the enemy. "If he wished to confer with him, it was permitted. He hoped that what pertained to the safety of the soldiers could be obtained from the people. To him, certainly, no injury would be done, and he pledged his faith to that effect." He consulted with Cotta, who had been wounded, about retiring from battle and conferring with Ambiorix.

While they negotiate on the terms, and a more extensive debate is undertaken by Ambiorix, who is surrounded by his allies, he is killed. Cotta, while fighting, was slain, together with the greater part of the soldiers. The rest retreated to the camp from which they had marched, and one of them, Lucius, remained behind. The standard bearer, Petrosidius, when overwhelmed by the large number of enemies, threw the eagle into the trenches and fell, fighting valiantly before the camp.

Several soldiers escaped from the battle and made their way to Labienus' winter quarters, where they wandered aimlessly through the woods before informing him of the events. As night fell, a large number of towers were erected in quick succession from the timber gathered for fortification purposes. The essential items required for the task were completed. The enemy attacked the camp the following day, having gathered a much larger force, and filled up the ditch. Our men are resisting in the same way as they did the day before. This same action is repeated over the remaining days.

The work is carried on incessantly in the night. Opportunity is not given even to the sick or wounded for rest. Whatever things are required to resist the assault of the next day are provided during the night. Many stakes are burned at the end, and a large number of mural pikes are procured. Towers are built up, battlements and parapets are formed from interwoven hurdles. XLIII On the seventh day of the attack, a strong wind had risen, prompting them to fire arrows made from hardened clay and heated javelins at the huts, which followed their customary practice of thatching with straw. This day was by far the most calamitous for our men. However, it had one notable result: the largest number of the enemy was wounded and killed, as they had crowded beneath the very rampart, with no room to retreat even for those at the rear.

Varenus remains outside the rampart, following the high opinion of all. When a considerable space intervened, Pulfio threw his javelin at the enemy, striking one of those who were running up. The wounded man was then covered by the enemy with their shields, and all the others hurled their weapons at him, leaving him no chance to retreat. Varenus rushed forward briskly, his sword at the ready. He engaged in close combat, slaying one opponent before driving back the others for a brief moment. However, as he pressed on too fiercely, he stumbled into a hollow and fell.

XLV As the attack intensified daily, its ferocity grew. With many soldiers wounded, the enemy's numbers dwindled, leaving only a small band of defenders. In response, more frequent letters and messengers were sent to Caesar, some of whom were captured and subjected to torture in front of our troops. He writes to Labienus, urging him to bring his legion to the frontiers of the Nervii, if it would benefit the commonwealth. He does not believe that waiting for the remaining portion of the army, which was farther away, would be advantageous; instead, he assembles about 400 horse from the nearest winter-quarters. He appoints Crassus over Samarobriva and assigns him a legion. He leaves behind the baggage of the army, the hostages of the states, the public documents, and all the grain that he had brought there to see them through the winter.

XLVIII Caesar approved of his motives, despite being disappointed by the reduction from three legions to two. He thus placed his sole reliance on swift dispatch for the common safety. He leads his army into the territories of the Nervii through long marches. He, after reading it, shares his findings with a group of soldiers, filling them with great joy.

As scouts were sent in all directions, he examined the most convenient path to cross the valley. L That day, skirmishes of cavalry had taken place near the river. Both armies remained in their own positions: the Gauls, awaiting larger forces that had not yet arrived; Caesar, to see if by pretence of fear he could lure the enemy towards his position, so that he might engage in battle, on this side of the valley, in front of his camp. If he could not accomplish this, then, having inquired about the passes, he would cross the valley and the river with less hazard. LII Caesar, fearing to pursue them too far, for woods and morasses intervened, reached Cicero on the same day with all his forces intact.

An assembly being held the following day, he states the occurrence; he consoles and encourages the soldiers. He suggests that the disaster, occasioned by his lieutenant's misconduct and rashness, should be borne with a patient mind, for it was the favour of the immortal gods and their own valour that neither left lasting joy to the enemy nor very lasting grief to them. LIII In the meantime, a report about Caesar's victory was sent to Labienus through the country of the Remi with incredible speed. Although he was approximately sixty miles away from Cicero's winter quarters, Caesar arrived after the ninth hour, and before midnight, a shout arose at the camp gates. This shout conveyed an indication of the victory and a congratulatory message from the Remi to Labienus. Caesar sent Fabius back with his legion to his winter quarters. He decided to spend the winter with three legions near Samarobriva, dividing them into three separate camps. This decision was prompted by the great commotion in Gaul, and he chose to remain with the army throughout the winter himself.

It is unclear what text was provided. None. Indutiomarus was disappointed with this expectation, yet he began to gather troops, discipline them, and acquire horses from neighboring people. He offered great rewards to lure outlaws and convicts from throughout Gaul. When our men failed to respond, the enemy, thinking it proper, departed towards evening in a disordered and scattered manner. Labienus unexpectedly sent out all the cavalry through two gates; he issued this command and prohibition that when the enemy should be terrified and put to flight (as he foresaw would happen, which did), they should all make for Indutiomarus, and no one was to wound any man until he had seen him slain, because he was unwilling that anyone should escape, in order to gain time by the delay caused by the pursuit of the rest.

After the affair became known, both the Eburones and the Nervii forces that had gathered departed. For a brief period following this event, Caesar faced fewer challenges in governing Gaul. And when Pompey had granted this to the interests of the commonwealth and the claims of friendship, Caesar having quickly completed the levy by means of his lieutenants, after three legions had been formed and brought to him before the winter expired, and the number of those cohorts which he had lost under Q. II The government was conferred upon his relatives by the Treviri after Indutiomarus' death.

III According to historical records, while winter had not yet concluded, he concentrated the four nearest legions and marched unexpectedly into the territories of the Nervii. Before they could assemble or retreat, he captured a large number of cattle and men, wasted their lands, and surrendered the booty to his soldiers. Compelled by this, the Nervii were forced to surrender and provide him with hostages. They were adjacent to the Senones and had united their state with theirs during the lifetime of their fathers, but were believed to have no part in the current plot. IV Acco, who had been the author of this enterprise, upon being informed of his arrival, ordered the people to assemble in the towns. Before this could be accomplished, news arrived that the Romans were close at hand: out of necessity, they abandoned their design and sent ambassadors to Caesar to plead for pardon. They made advances to him through the Aedui, a state that had been under Roman protection since ancient times.

Caesar readily granted them pardon and received their excuse at the request of the Aedui. He did so because he believed the summer season was more suitable for an impending war than for an investigation. As he had arranged everything, considering it certain that Ambiorix would not engage in battle, he carefully observed the rest of his plans. He, after receiving hostages, assures them that he will consider them among his enemies if they shall receive either Ambiorix or his ambassadors within their territories.

VII While these things are being performed by Caesar, the Treviri drew together large forces of infantry and cavalry to attack Labienus and the legion wintering in their territories. They were already not further distant from him than a journey of two days when they learned that two legions had arrived at his order. Labienus, having learned the design of the enemy, hoped that their rashness would create an opportunity to engage. He left a guard of five cohorts for the baggage and then advanced against the enemy with twenty cohorts and a large body of cavalry. A space of about a mile separated them, after which he fortified his camp.
